dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a 65-year-old unconscious female with diabetic history.
Good evening, I'm Jess, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
My wife was lying in bed. I found her unresponsive when I woke from our nap.
Hey ma'am. Can you open your eyes for me? Do you know where you are right now?
She isn't opening her eyes. No eye opening detected.
Do you mind if we try again? Can you tell me your name or where you are?
No verbal or motor response observed. She seems unresponsive to all stimulation.
She has shallow, snoring-type, agonal respirations. Attaching BVM now for assisted breathing.
She has a weak central pulse, and her skin is cool, clammy, and intact.
Her pulse is 54 at the scene. It's slow but it's still detectable.
Respiratory rate is 16, a little irregular but otherwise somewhat stable.
Blood pressure is 140 over 82. That's within normal range but perhaps peripherally diminished.
Glucose is 15, indicating hypoglycemia. We'll be working to raise it now.
SpO2 is 100% on 15 liters of oxygen via BVM. She's well-oxygenated at the moment.
EKG findings show sinus bradycardia. No other cardiac rhythm changes noticeable.
Mr. Thomas, could you confirm any medical conditions your wife has?
She has Type 1 diabetes. She takes insulin daily, and hasn't eaten anything today.
Can you tell me when she had insulin and how her day went leading up to this event?
She had a high glucose this morning. She took her insulin later this afternoon, and skipped meals.
Has she shown any recent chest pain, nausea, or other symptoms prior to being found unresponsive?
No, she was unresponsive with no signs of distress when I checked on her.
Are there any known allergies we should be aware of?
No known allergies.
What other medications has she been taking?
She's on Humalog Mix for insulin management.
Got it. I'll note that and continue with our management.
Are you seeing any pain indicators like grimacing or abnormal movements?
No signs of pain. I'd rate it as a 0.
Her skin is cool, clammy, and there are no rashes, bruises, or burns noted.
Pupils are equal, round, reactive to light and accommodation, but slightly sluggish.
Trachea is midline, no jugular venous distension, and no masses detected in the neck.
Clear bilateral lung sounds, no wheezes or crackles detected.
Placing the patient on a cardiac monitor with 4-lead placement for ongoing assessment.
Abdomen is soft, non-tender, and non-distended. Pelvis is stable with no bruising observed.
No deformities, angulation, or motor deficits seen on extremities or back.
The 12-lead ECG shows sinus bradycardia with nonspecific T-wave changes and prolonged QT interval.
Mr. Thomas, we are starting with establishing vascular access. First step is to attempt intravenous access.
First IV attempt in extremity vein (arm) made, no success. Switching location now.
Four separate IV attempts in extremity veins (two arms and two legs) unsuccessful due to no venous return.
We are now attempting intraosseous access as an alternative route.
Intraosseous access successfully established in the left humeral head.
125 ml of 10% dextrose solution administered via the intraosseous route.
We also administered 1 mg of glucagon via intramuscular route for additional glucose support.
We are following EMS protocol 3-8 for hypoglycemia due to diabetes.
Current pulse is 55 and still bradycardic. Her response seems consistent.
Blood pressure has decreased to 74 over 50. It's trending downward.
Respiratory rate is remaining at 16 per minute with consistent pattern.
SpO2 has dropped slightly to 99%, still high on BVM.
Glucose level is 32, still low, and trending lower.
EKG still shows pattern of sinus bradycardia with the same T wave and QT interval findings.
Mr. Thomas, she seems stable but has no clinical improvement in responsiveness yet.
She was in bed when I found her and showed no signs of distress before this.
No response observed to medications. We'll maintain current support until transport.
Pulse now remains at 55. No significant changes in vitals.
Blood pressure is 99 over 83. There's some slight improvement we can note.
SpO2 is 97%, and still well within acceptable range for now.
Glucose is now 29. Finally showing a small reversal in the trend so far.
Transporting the patient to VCU Health for definitive long-term medical care.
Patient is moving to a Reeves stretcher and will be placed in semi-fowler's position.
Secured with 5 seat belts to ensure stability during transport.
Descending from the apartment through five steps with physical assistance.
The patient is now supine in the stretcher and ready for ambulance placement.
Patient is securely in the semi-fowler's position in the ambulance and fully stabilized.
En route to VCU Health Systems with continuous monitoring and care.
I'll provide a full report to the receiving nurse once we arrive at the ER.
Patient will be placed in trauma bed 3 and taken care of by Tenesha Bottoms, RN.
She remains in semi-fowler's position for transport and airway stability.
